reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest values: manageable workload, fair compensation for added administrative/invigilation duties, and professional voice in assessment design — grounded in the documented Hungarian teacher shortage (OECD Education at a Glance; PSZ/PDSZ workload surveys, as cited in the S4 evidence base) that leaves little slack for unfunded new duties.
reactions.interest: Teachers currently working in existing 6-/8-year gimnazium and feeder general schools who would administer, invigilate, or be audited under the new assessment and quota regime
reactions.public_good_frame: A teaching workforce not stretched by unfunded new administrative tasks delivers better instruction to all pupils, including those the reform aims to help; loading compliance work onto classrooms without resourcing degrades the very intake process the reform is meant to improve.
reactions.argument: S1 preserves existing school identities, staff, and buildings, which protects current teacher job security compared to structural alternatives. But the assessment redesign/pilot and the new compliance-audit unit add administration, invigilation, and monitoring work that is not currently costed as classroom-level time; if it lands on already short-staffed schools without dedicated pay or support staff, it is an unfunded workload increase. The assessment itself is designed by Oktatási Hivatal/psychometric bodies, not by teachers, so this scenario does not by itself expand teacher professional autonomy over what or how they assess.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support fully if any new administration/monitoring duties are matched with dedicated compensation or non-teaching staff, and teachers are consulted during design and piloting; would move to oppose if compliance auditing is used to sanction individual teachers or schools for quota shortfalls without addressing the underlying causes (e.g., applicant-pool composition) they do not control.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest values: staffing feasibility and workload sustainability when instructional demands change — the scenario's own uncertainty section names 'current teacher shortages' as the reason general-school absorption of higher-achieving pupils is unproven, which is the value basis for this position.
reactions.interest: General-school teachers who would be expected to deliver advanced-programme instruction as intake shifts, and gimnazium teachers in the shrinking selective track whose future post levels are not addressed by the plan
reactions.public_good_frame: Whether mixed-ability classrooms actually raise the ceiling for able pupils without lowering it for others depends on whether teachers are given the training, time, and staffing to differentiate instruction — a workforce capacity question, not just a funding-formula question.
reactions.argument: The scenario's own uncertainty section concedes that general schools' ability to challenge high-achievers 'given current teacher shortages' is unresolved (confidence: low). Ramping advanced-programme funding on a 10-year phased schedule without a parallel teacher-supply and training pipeline risks asking already-stretched general-school staff to take on more demanding, differentiated instruction without matching recruitment or preparation. On the selective-track side, felmenő rendszer protects currently enrolled pupils' teaching arrangements for the transition cohort, but the plan states no guarantee about post levels or redeployment support for staff in that track once caps tighten in later years.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if the phased funding plan is paired with a concrete teacher-recruitment/training pipeline sized to the pace of the cap trajectory, and with an explicit redeployment or retraining guarantee for staff in the shrinking selective track; would oppose if caps tighten faster than staffing capacity is demonstrably built, since that shifts complexity onto classrooms without support.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest values: adequately resourced and voluntary skills transitions, job/post security during structural change, and professional autonomy over pedagogical approach — applied to the scenario's own listed cost item of 'nationwide teacher retraining for differentiated instruction' (high cost, no numeric estimate) and its 6-8 year implementation horizon.
reactions.interest: All classroom teachers subject to nationwide differentiated-instruction retraining, and teachers in currently-selective 6-/8-year tracks facing institutional conversion to upper-secondary-only status
reactions.public_good_frame: A comprehensive-school reform can only deliver its equity promise if teachers are actually equipped and willing to teach mixed-ability classrooms well; retraining imposed without adequate resourcing or timeline certainty risks producing the appearance of reform without the instructional capacity behind it, which serves no pupil.
reactions.argument: The scenario lists nationwide differentiated-instruction retraining as a high, unestimated one-off cost, and its own implementation steps schedule pilot retraining, then pilot rollout, then national scale-up and legislative conversion 6-8 years out — a long window in which affected teachers have no clarity on whether their posts, subjects, or schools will convert, be merged, or be phased out. The scenario nowhere specifies whether retraining is compulsory or voluntary, salary-linked, or credential-bearing, nor whether teachers in converting institutions are guaranteed continued employment rather than redeployment on worse terms. This proceeds against a background of documented Hungarian teacher shortage, which the scenario itself does not address.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support if retraining is fully funded, credential- or salary-linked, offered with adequate lead time and choice rather than compulsion, and bundled with an explicit no-worse-off guarantee on post, pay, and location for teachers in converting institutions; would oppose the national conversion step proceeding on the stated 6-8 year timeline if teacher-supply and retraining capacity have not been demonstrated in the pilot districts first.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest values: adequate and secure pay, and staffing feasibility in shortage conditions — directly engaging the scenario's own strong-evidence claim (OECD Education at a Glance; PSZ/PDSZ surveys) that 'money alone cannot manufacture qualified teachers in a shortage market,' and its political-risk finding that the compensation budget line is fiscally exposed to being cut first.
reactions.interest: Teachers currently working or considering working in disadvantaged general schools eligible for TEIP-style premia, recruitment incentives, and clustering-based administrative support
reactions.public_good_frame: Pay premia and support packages only improve outcomes in disadvantaged schools if they are durable enough for teachers to plan a career around; a promise that can be withdrawn in the next fiscal downturn does not build the stable, resourced workforce disadvantaged pupils need.
reactions.argument: Of the four scenarios, S4 engages teacher material interests most directly — funding premia, recruitment incentives, and clustering to ease administrative load in disadvantaged schools — and avoids the retraining/redeployment disruption of S2 and S3. But the scenario's own political-risk section states, at high-confidence evidence, that the teacher shortage limits what premia alone can achieve, and separately flags the compensation funding line as the first target in fiscal consolidation, with only an untested multi-year budget-line proposal as protection. A teacher who takes up a disadvantaged-school post on the strength of a premium that is later cut is worse off than if no premium had existed, having built career and location plans around it.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support fully if premia and support packages are protected by a legally durable, multi-year funding commitment rather than an annual budget line, and paired with active supply-side measures (expanded training places, credential pathways) rather than pay alone; would oppose enacting premia without that protection, since an unprotected promise creates a worse staffing outcome than making none.
